text: During the laparoscopic right hemicolectomy, the surgeon elevates the transverse colon and mesocolon to expose the middle colic artery. The small bowel is pushed to the left lower quadrant by tilting the table, revealing the retroperitoneum. The surgeon identifies the iliocolic trunk at its junction with the superior mesenteric vessels, elevating the ileocolic junction to create a groove beneath the vessels. A lymph node is visible, and the peritoneum is opened to allow gas to dissect the plane, aiming to expose the duodenum for safe ligation of the iliocolic trunk.

The surgeon uses a grasper to elevate the ileocolic vessels, ligating them at their origin from the superior mesenteric vessels. The duodenum is visible, dissected downwards, while the superior mesenteric vessels are pulled due to traction. The ileocolic vein and artery are ligated, with lymph nodes and lymphatics included in the specimen.

The surgeon applies upward traction on the ileocolic vessel stump to develop the medial to lateral dissection plane. The dissection proceeds between Toldt's fascia and the mesocolon, continuing until reaching the iliopsoas muscle, indicating the end of the lateral dissection. The retroperitoneal structures remain protected beneath Toldt's fascia. The dissection is carried cephalad underneath the colon, and the peritoneum superior to the colon and beneath the liver is opened to complete the dissection of the colon and its mesentery.

The panoramic view shows the colon and its mesentery mobilized from medial to lateral, with only lateral paracolic attachments remaining. The retroperitoneum has been safely dissected away. The dissection progresses upwards towards the duodenum and head of the pancreas, carefully separating the head of the pancreas from the mesocolon to avoid injury. The head of the pancreas is mobilized downwards to join the duodenum, exposing the right colic vessels. The gallbladder is visible through the peritoneum.

The surgeon displays the ileocolic pedicle, including its lymphatics, and proceeds to cut across the ileal mesentery. The ileum is transected using staplers with white cartridges, completing the inferior portion of the surgery. The procedure then progresses upwards to transect the colon and remove the specimen, followed by performing the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization and cuts the paracolic peritoneum, facilitating the removal of the specimen. The transverse colon is brought downwards, and the gastrocolic omentum is opened to separate the colon from the greater omentum, preparing the transverse colon for transection.

The surgical field is shown after the removal of the specimen, revealing the transected colon and its mesentery. The surgeon prepares for an anastomosis by aligning the terminal ileum and transverse colon in an iso-peristaltic fashion. A stay suture is used to fix the ileum and colon together, followed by a demonstration of a loose knot technique to bring the two structures into proximity for the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an ileocolic anastomosis using a six-centimeter blue cartridge stapler. The enterotomies are then closed with a running PDS 3-0 suture, ensuring secure closure of the intestinal openings.
this concludes our technique for live image collecting and laparoscopy
